english: Heinrich calls in a panic: Spargelzeit ends June 24! Learn the difference between 'als' and 'wenn,' plus food shopping and nutrition vocabulary.
Understand the German obsession with Spargelzeit
Learn als (one-time past) vs wenn (repeated/conditional)
Pick up food shopping and nutrition vocabulary
Hildi's phone buzzes. She puts it on speaker. Heinrich's voice fills the whole table. He sounds agitated.
It is the twentieth of June! Asparagus season ends on the twenty-fourth! Have you ordered asparagus yet? I insist!
Why is asparagus so urgent? It is just a vegetable...
When I was a child, we waited every year for the first asparagus. When the season ends, there is no more. Until next year.
Heinrich is right. If you miss the last asparagus, that is a national tragedy.
Hildi orders six portions of asparagus. With butter and potatoes. Heinrich hears it through the speaker and sighs contentedly. Then he hangs up without saying goodbye.
the season (food/sports)
From French 'saison.' Spargelzeit is Germany's most famous food Saison.

A: When is strawberry season?
B: From May to July.
A: And asparagus season?
B: Mid-April to the twenty-fourth of June.
From French 'saison.' Spargelzeit is Germany's most famous food Saison: mid-April to June 24. Germans eat 1.7 kg of Spargel per person per year.
the harvest
From 'ernten' (to harvest). 'Erntezeit' (harvest time) is peak season for farmers.

A: The harvest was good this year.
B: Yes, the summer was perfect.
A: Especially the asparagus harvest.
B: The best in years!
Harvest. From 'ernten' (to harvest). 'Erntezeit' (harvest time) is when German farmers are busiest. The word carries weight in a country where seasonal eating is still important.
the receipt
From Latin 'quietare' (to settle). Germans keep Quittungen for everything.

A: Can I have the receipt?
B: Of course, here you go.
A: I need it for the tax return.
B: Typically German!
From Latin 'quietare' (to settle). 'Kann ich die Quittung haben?' is essential shopping vocabulary. Germans keep Quittungen for everything, especially for tax returns.
the discount
From Italian 'rabatto.' A discount or reduction.

A: Is there a discount on the asparagus?
B: Yes, ten percent, because the season is almost over.
A: Then I will take two kilos!
B: Smart decision.
From Italian 'rabatto' (discount). 'Sonderangebot' (special offer) and 'Rabatt' are the words that make German shoppers happy.
the special offer
'Sonder' (special) + 'Angebot' (offer). Neuter because Angebot is neuter.

A: Look, asparagus on special offer!
B: Only three euros per kilo!
A: Heinrich would be thrilled.
B: Call him!
'Sonder' (special) + 'Angebot' (offer). German supermarkets advertise Sonderangebote in weekly flyers. Das Sonderangebot is neuter because Angebot is neuter. Compound gender rule.
the snake, the queue
'Schlange stehen' (to stand in line) makes the queue a snake.

A: Oh no, the queue is so long!
B: Yes, everyone wants the last asparagus.
A: We have to stand in line.
B: That is part of German daily life.
A snake AND a queue. 'Schlange stehen' (to stand in line) makes the queue a snake. Germans queue precisely but not always patiently.
the oven
'Back' (bake) + 'Ofen' (oven). Every German kitchen has one.

A: Preheat the oven to 180 degrees.
B: Top and bottom heat?
A: Yes, for the asparagus casserole.
B: It will be hot in twenty minutes.
'Back' (bake) + 'Ofen' (oven). 'Den Backofen auf 180 Grad vorheizen' (Preheat the oven to 180 degrees) begins every German recipe. Celsius, naturally.
the foodstuff
'Nahrung' (nourishment) + 'Mittel' (means). The formal, official word for food.

A: Foodstuffs must be labeled correctly.
B: That is the law.
A: Every ingredient must be on the packaging.
B: Germany takes that seriously.
'Nahrung' (nourishment) + 'Mittel' (means/agent). The formal, official word for food. You see it on packaging and in regulations. More technical than 'Essen' (food/eating).
the nutrition, the diet
From 'ernähren' (to nourish). Covers your entire relationship with food.

A: Do you watch your diet?
B: Yes, I eat lots of fruit and vegetables.
A: Healthy nutrition is important.
B: Especially in summer with fresh asparagus!
From 'ernähren' (to nourish). 'Gesunde Ernährung' (healthy nutrition) is a topic Germans take seriously. Die Ernährung covers your entire relationship with food.
the diet
From Greek 'diaita' (way of life). Can also mean a special medical diet.

A: Are you on a diet?
B: No, I just eat less sugar.
A: That is also a kind of diet.
B: True, but it does not feel like it.
From Greek 'diaita' (way of life). In German, Diät can also mean a special medical diet, not just weight loss. Politicians' salaries are also called 'Diäten.'
the vitamin
Coined in 1912 from Latin 'vita' (life) + 'amine.' 'Vitamin C' is pronounced 'Vitamin Tsay.'

A: Asparagus has many vitamins.
B: Especially vitamin C and K.
A: That is why it is so healthy.
B: And delicious!
Coined in 1912 from Latin 'vita' (life) + 'amine.' Germans are vitamin-conscious. 'Vitamin C' is pronounced 'Vitamin Tsay.' The health-food market in Germany is enormous.
ripe, mature
'Der Apfel ist reif.' Also figurative: 'reif für etwas sein' (to be ready for something).

A: Are the strawberries ripe yet?
B: Yes, they are perfectly red.
A: Then we will pick them today.
B: The ripe ones are the sweetest.
Ripe, mature. Also used figuratively: 'reif für etwas sein' (to be ready for something). 'Ich bin reif für den Urlaub' (I am ready for vacation).
durable, having a shelf life
'Halt' (hold) + '-bar' (able). 'Mindestens haltbar bis...' (Best before...).

A: How long does the milk keep?
B: Until next Wednesday.
A: And the canned goods?
B: They keep for years.
'Halt' (hold) + '-bar' (able). 'Mindestens haltbar bis...' (Best before...) appears on every German food package. The -bar suffix works like English '-able.'
vegetarian
From Latin 'vegetare' (to enliven). Germany has high rates of vegetarianism.

A: Is there anything vegetarian?
B: Yes, potato salad and asparagus soup.
A: Perfect, I do not eat meat.
B: In Berlin that is completely normal.
From Latin 'vegetare' (to enliven). Germany has one of the highest rates of vegetarianism in Europe. Berlin alone has over 60 fully vegetarian restaurants.
to prepare (food)
'Zu' (to) + 'bereiten' (to prepare). More specific than 'vorbereiten.' Separable.

A: How do you prepare asparagus?
B: Peel, boil, serve with butter.
A: How long does it cook?
B: Ten to fifteen minutes.
'Zu' (to) + 'bereiten' (to prepare). More specific than 'vorbereiten' (general preparation). Separable: 'Ich bereite das Essen zu.'
to mix
Regular verb. 'Einen Salat mischen' (to toss a salad). Related to English 'mix.'

A: Can you toss the salad?
B: Sure, with which dressing?
A: Vinegar and oil.
B: I will mix everything together.
To mix. Regular verb. 'Einen Salat mischen' (to toss a salad), 'Karten mischen' (to shuffle cards). Related to English 'mix' through Latin 'miscere.'
to rinse, to wash (dishes)
'Geschirr spülen' (to wash dishes). Also 'die Toilette spülen' (to flush).

A: Who washes the dishes today?
B: You! I washed them yesterday.
A: We need a dishwasher.
B: True, hand-washing takes forever.
To rinse or wash (dishes). 'Geschirr spülen' (to wash dishes). Also 'die Toilette spülen' (to flush the toilet). The Spülmaschine is the dishwasher.

German has two words where English has one.

ALS = one specific moment in the past.
'Als ich ein Kind war...' (When I was a child...)
'Als Heinrich anrief...' (When Heinrich called...)

WENN = every time, repeatedly, or hypothetically.
'Wenn ich Spargel esse...' (When/whenever I eat asparagus...)
'Wenn die Saison endet...' (When the season ends...)

Memory trick:
ALS = ONCE in the past.
WENN = WHENEVER or IF.

Hildi says 'Als ich ein Kind war' (one specific past). Lukas says 'Wenn man den letzten Spargel verpasst' (every year, repeated).

The distinction is strict in German. You cannot use 'wenn' for a single past event, and you cannot use 'als' for repeated or present events. In reported speech or written German, the rule holds firm. Some learners think 'als' means 'as' and 'wenn' means 'when/if,' but the real key is: was it a one-time past event (als) or is it repeated/habitual/conditional (wenn)?
Als = one specific past event. Wenn = repeated, habitual, or hypothetical.
Yesterday is a single, specific moment in the past. Which word means 'when' for one-time events?
The asparagus season ends EVERY year. This is a repeated event.
When the asparagus season ends, Germans are always sad.
Some German verbs always come with a specific preposition. Learn the combos, then replace them with clever da- and wo- compounds.
Master verbs with fixed prepositions (sich freuen auf/über, warten auf, denken an)
Learn da-compounds (darauf, darüber) to replace prepositional phrases
Form questions with wo-compounds (worauf, worüber)
What are you most looking forward to right now, Verumius? To summer? To the food? Or are you waiting for something else?
I am happy about this evening. And I think of home. Sometimes I am interested in everything at the same time.
the consumption
From Latin 'consumere.' Covers everything from food to media consumption.

A: The consumption of asparagus increases every year.
B: Germans love asparagus.
A: The consumption of organic products is also growing.
B: Yes, more and more people buy organic.
From Latin 'consumere.' 'Konsumgesellschaft' (consumer society) is a word Germans use critically. Der Konsum is masculine.
the liquid, the fluid
'Flüssig' (liquid) + '-keit' (noun suffix). The -keit suffix always creates feminine nouns.

A: Do you drink enough fluids?
B: I try. Two liters a day.
A: In this heat you need more.
B: True, I will get more water.
'Flüssig' (liquid) + '-keit' (noun suffix). 'Viel Flüssigkeit trinken' (drink plenty of fluids) is standard German health advice. The -keit suffix always creates feminine nouns.
the alcohol
From Arabic 'al-kuhl' (the kohl/essence). Germany has relaxed alcohol laws.

A: Do you drink alcohol?
B: Sometimes. A beer in the beer garden.
A: In Germany you can drink beer from age sixteen.
B: Yes, but spirits only from eighteen.
From Arabic 'al-kuhl' (the kohl/essence). Germany has some of the most relaxed alcohol laws in Europe. Beer from age 16, spirits from 18.
the cocoa, the hot chocolate
From Nahuatl 'cacahuatl' via Spanish. 'Jemanden durch den Kakao ziehen' = to make fun of someone.

A: Would you like cocoa or coffee?
B: Cocoa, please. With cream.
A: You always drink cocoa!
B: Yes, that is my favorite drink.
From Nahuatl 'cacahuatl' via Spanish. 'Jemanden durch den Kakao ziehen' (to pull someone through the cocoa) means to make fun of someone.
the service employee (female)
'Service' + 'Angestellte' (employee). Gender-neutral alternative to Kellner/Kellnerin.

A: Excuse me, can you help us?
B: Of course! I am your service attendant this evening.
A: We would like the menu.
B: Right away!
'Service' + 'Angestellte' (employee). The gender-neutral alternative to Kellner/Kellnerin. Modern German hospitality vocabulary.
the rental, the letting
From 'vermieten' (to rent out). Feminine because of -ung.

A: The rental of vacation apartments is booming.
B: Especially in Berlin.
A: Do you see the sign? 'Rental from June.'
B: Maybe for the summer.
From 'vermieten' (to rent out). 'Zimmervermietung' (room rental). Die Vermietung is feminine because of -ung. You see it on signs everywhere.
the mediation, the placement
From 'vermitteln' (to mediate/arrange). The -ung ending makes it feminine.

A: The agency found me an apartment.
B: How quickly?
A: In just two weeks.
B: That is a miracle in Berlin!
From 'vermitteln' (to mediate/arrange). 'Arbeitsvermittlung' (job placement), 'Zimmervermittlung' (accommodation service). The -ung ending makes it feminine.
the opening, the inauguration
From 'eröffnen' (to open formally). A formal event with speeches and ribbon-cutting.

A: Are you coming to the opening of the new beer garden?
B: Yes, of course! When is it?
A: Next Saturday at noon.
B: I will be there!
From 'eröffnen' (to open formally). 'Die Eröffnung des Biergartens' (the opening of the beer garden). Grand openings in Germany involve speeches and ribbon-cutting.
organic
Short for 'biologisch.' 'Bio-Bratwurst,' 'Bio-Bier.' Germany is Europe's largest organic market.

A: Is the bratwurst organic?
B: Yes, from an organic farm.
A: Then I will take two.
B: Organic is more expensive, but it is worth it.
Short for 'biologisch.' Germany is the largest organic food market in Europe. The 'Bio' label is legally protected and strictly regulated.
biological, organic
From Greek 'bios' (life) + 'logos' (study). In food context it means organic.

A: Is that organically grown?
B: Yes, without pesticides.
A: Organic farming is important.
B: For the environment and for us.
From Greek 'bios' (life) + 'logos' (study). 'Biologische Landwirtschaft' (organic farming). Longer form of 'bio,' used in formal or written contexts.
lean, thin, low-fat
'Magermilch' (skim milk), 'mageres Fleisch' (lean meat).

A: Do you have skim milk?
B: No, only whole milk.
A: I watch my diet.
B: Then I will buy skim milk next time.
Lean, thin, low-fat. 'Magermilch' (skim milk), 'mageres Fleisch' (lean meat). Not a compliment when applied to food portions.
sharp, pointed
'Ein spitzer Stift' (a sharp pencil). Also 'spitze!' as slang for 'great!'

A: Careful, the knife is sharp!
B: Thanks, I will be careful.
A: And how was the food?
B: Great!
Sharp, pointed. 'Ein spitzer Stift' (a sharp pencil). Also 'spitze!' as slang for 'great!' Related to English 'spit' (the pointed landform).
to open (formally), to inaugurate
'Er-' (to begin/achieve) + 'öffnen' (to open). More formal than simply 'öffnen.'

A: When does the new beer garden open?
B: Next week.
A: Who officially opens it?
B: The mayor!
'Er-' (to begin/achieve) + 'öffnen' (to open). You 'eröffnen' a business, a meeting, or a Biergarten season. More formal than simply 'öffnen' (to open a door).
to consume
From Latin 'consumere.' More formal than 'essen' or 'trinken.'

A: Germans consume a lot of beer.
B: But less than before.
A: Consumption has been dropping for years.
B: But wine consumption is increasing.
From Latin 'consumere.' More formal than 'essen' or 'trinken.' 'Alkohol konsumieren' sounds more official. Used in health contexts and regulations.
to solve, to dissolve, to buy (a ticket)
Three meanings in one verb. 'Ein Problem lösen,' 'eine Fahrkarte lösen.'

A: I still need to buy a ticket.
B: At the machine or online?
A: Online is easier.
B: Problem solved!
To solve, to dissolve, to buy (a ticket). 'Ein Problem lösen,' 'Salz löst sich in Wasser,' 'eine Fahrkarte lösen.' Three meanings, one verb.
to lift, to raise
Strong verb: heben, hob, gehoben. 'Ein Glas heben' (to raise a glass). Related to English 'heave.'

A: Let us raise our glasses!
B: Cheers! To summer!
A: This beer glass is heavy.
B: One liter! That is exercise.
Strong verb: heben, hob, gehoben. 'Ein Glas heben' (to raise a glass) is what you do at 'Prost!' Related to English 'heave.'

Some German verbs always pair with a specific preposition. The preposition changes the meaning.

sich freuen AUF = look forward to (future)
sich freuen ÜBER = be happy about (present/past)
warten AUF = wait for
denken AN = think of
bestehen AUF = insist on
sich interessieren FÜR = be interested in

You cannot swap the prepositions. Each combo is one fixed unit. Memorize them as pairs.

Example from the story:
Heinrich besteht AUF Spargel. (Heinrich insists ON asparagus.)
Verumius freut sich AUF den Abend. (Verumius looks forward TO the evening.)

The case after the preposition matters too. 'Auf' with fixed-preposition verbs usually takes accusative: 'Ich warte auf DEN Kellner.' 'An' takes accusative with 'denken': 'Ich denke an DEN Abend.' These differ from the spatial use of the same prepositions, where the two-way prepositions take dative for location and accusative for direction.
Each verb-preposition pair is a fixed unit. The preposition determines the case.

When you want to say 'about it' or 'for it,' German combines da- with a preposition:
- darauf (on it), darüber (about it), daran (of it), dafür (for it)

For questions, combine wo- with a preposition:
- worauf (on what), worüber (about what), woran (of what), wofür (for what)

When the preposition starts with a vowel, an -r- is inserted for smoother pronunciation:
- da- plus auf becomes darauf
- wo- plus über becomes worüber

Example:
'Worauf wartest du?' -- 'Darauf, dass der Kellner kommt.'
(What are you waiting for? -- For the waiter to come.)

Da-compounds replace prepositional phrases that refer to things (not people). For people, use the preposition plus a pronoun: 'Ich denke an ihn' (I think of him), NOT 'Ich denke daran' when referring to a person. Wo-compounds work the same way: 'An wen denkst du?' (Who are you thinking of?) for people, 'Woran denkst du?' (What are you thinking of?) for things.
Looking forward to something in the FUTURE uses 'sich freuen' plus the preposition for future anticipation.
The verb is 'bestehen AUF.' Replace 'auf Spargel' with da + auf.
The answer uses 'auf.' The question word combines 'wo' with 'auf.'
What are you looking forward to? -- To summer!
The FIRST time is a single, one-time past event. Which word is for that?
First: the verb for being interested plus its fixed preposition. Second: 'denken an' replaced with a da-compound.
Mia is interested in organic food. She often thinks about it.
The golden hour fades to blue. Fairy lights glow. Lukas picks up his guitar. His band broke up last month. Tonight, he plays alone.
Practice als vs wenn in emotional contexts
Use verbs with fixed prepositions to describe feelings
Learn nature and agriculture vocabulary
The golden hour fades to blue. Fairy lights come alive in the chestnut branches. Lukas stands on the small raised area, guitar in hand. No one asked him to play. He just started.
She leans toward Verumius: 'His band broke up last month. He does not talk about it. But he thinks about it constantly.'
Lukas plays. Eyes closed. The music is beautiful and sad. A song about summer evenings that will not last. Hildi puts down her beer stein. Even she is quiet.
When I first met him, he was always funny. Now I understand: when he makes music, he shows what he really feels.
Lukas sits down. Hildi slides a beer toward him without a word. At the next table someone claps. Then another. Lukas smiles, properly for the first time this evening.
the meadow
'Liegewiese' (sunbathing lawn) is where Germans spread blankets in parks.

A: Let us sit on the meadow.
B: Yes, the grass is soft.
A: The meadow is full of flowers.
B: A perfect summer evening.
A meadow. 'Liegewiese' (sunbathing lawn) is where Germans spread their blankets in parks. 'Auf der grünen Wiese' is also business jargon for building from scratch.
the grass
Same word and same root as English. 'Das Gras wachsen hören' = to be overly perceptive.

A: The grass in the park is so green.
B: Yes, it rained a lot.
A: Perfect for a picnic.
B: But not too wet?
Same word and same root as English. 'Das Gras wachsen hören' (to hear the grass grow) means to be overly sensitive or perceptive.
the field
Cognate with English 'field.' Germany's agricultural Felder feed much of Europe.

A: Look at the fields!
B: Grain, as far as you can see.
A: The harvest will be good this year.
B: Yes, the rain was perfect.
Cognate with English 'field.' 'Feldforschung' (field research), 'Spielfeld' (playing field). Germany's agricultural Felder feed much of Europe.
the cloth, the scarf
'Halstuch' (neck scarf), 'Handtuch' (towel), 'Tischtuch' (tablecloth).

A: Do you have a scarf with you?
B: Yes, my neck scarf. Why?
A: It is getting cooler in the evening.
B: True, take it.
A cloth or scarf. German compounds with Tuch name every type of fabric you drape: 'Halstuch' (neck scarf), 'Handtuch' (hand-cloth = towel), 'Tischtuch' (tablecloth).
the sack, the bag
Same word as English. In Austria: 'Sackerl' replaces 'Tüte' for a small bag.

A: Please bring a sack of potatoes.
B: From the market or the supermarket?
A: From the market, they are fresher.
B: Good, I will take a big sack.
Same word as English. In Austria and southern Germany, 'Sackerl' replaces 'Tüte' for a small bag. 'Die Katze aus dem Sack lassen' (let the cat out of the bag) works in German too.
the can, the tin, the box
'Konservenbüchse' (tin can). In some regions, Büchse and Dose are interchangeable.

A: Open the can of beans.
B: Where is the can opener?
A: In the drawer.
B: Found it!
A can, tin, or box. 'Konservenbüchse' (tin can). In some regions, Büchse and Dose are interchangeable. Also historically a rifle or musket, from the cylindrical shape.
the dumpling
Plural: Klöße. In southern Germany: Knödel. Classic side dish.

A: Do you like dumplings?
B: Yes! Potato dumplings are my favorite side.
A: In Bavaria they say Knödel.
B: Equally delicious, no matter what you call them.
A dumpling. Plural: Klöße. In southern Germany: Knödel. 'Einen Kloß im Hals haben' (to have a dumpling in your throat) means to be choked up with emotion.
the cook, the chef
From Latin 'coquus.' 'Viele Köche verderben den Brei' (Too many cooks spoil the porridge).

A: The chef here is fantastic.
B: Yes, everything tastes perfect.
A: He has been cooking for twenty years.
B: Experience makes the chef.
From Latin 'coquus.' 'Viele Köche verderben den Brei' (Too many cooks spoil the porridge). Same proverb, same meaning, same ancient wisdom.
the cook, the chef (female)
Koch + -in. More women lead professional kitchens in Germany now.

A: The chef has a new recipe.
B: What is there today?
A: Asparagus soup with bread rolls.
B: Sounds wonderful!
Female cook. Koch + -in. More and more women lead professional kitchens in Germany, though the industry still has a long way to go.
the agriculture
'Land' (country/land) + 'Wirtschaft' (economy). Feminine because of -schaft.

A: Agriculture in Brandenburg is important.
B: Yes, many farms there.
A: Asparagus often comes from Brandenburg.
B: The best asparagus region in Germany!
'Land' (country/land) + 'Wirtschaft' (economy/management). Germany's agricultural sector feeds 80 million people efficiently.
to wake up
'Auf' (up) + 'wachen' (to be awake). Separable: 'Ich wache um sechs auf.'

A: When did you wake up?
B: At six o'clock. The birds woke me.
A: In summer I also wake up early.
B: The light comes so early!
'Auf' (up) + 'wachen' (to be awake). Not to be confused with 'aufwecken' (to wake someone else up). Aufwachen is what you do. Aufwecken is what your alarm does to you.
to bloom, to blossom
From the same root as 'Blume' (flower) and 'Blüte' (blossom).

A: The roses are blooming beautifully.
B: Yes, the garden looks great.
A: In spring everything blooms.
B: My favorite time!
To bloom, to blossom. Also figurative: 'Das Geschäft blüht' (Business is blooming). From the same root as 'Blume' (flower) and 'Blüte' (blossom).
to approach, to come closer
Often reflexive: 'sich nähern.' From 'nah' (near).

A: We are approaching the beer garden.
B: I can already smell the bratwurst!
A: Five more minutes.
B: I can hardly wait.
To approach, to come closer. Often reflexive: 'sich nähern.' 'Wir nähern uns dem Ziel' (We are approaching the goal). From 'nah' (near).
to plant
From Latin 'planta.' 'Einen Baum pflanzen.' Also a noun: die Pflanze (the plant).

A: Did you plant the tomatoes?
B: Yes, yesterday afternoon.
A: When can we harvest?
B: In about two months.
From Latin 'planta.' Germans take gardening seriously: Kleingärten (allotment gardens) are a national institution.
to grow
Strong verb: wachsen, wuchs, gewachsen. Not to be confused with 'wachsen' (to wax).

A: Asparagus grows under the ground.
B: That is why it is white?
A: Exactly! Without light it stays white.
B: Fascinating!
Strong verb: wachsen, wuchs, gewachsen. 'Kinder wachsen schnell.' Not to be confused with 'wachsen' (to wax), which is a different verb with different origins.
to pour, to water (plants)
Strong verb: goss, gegossen. 'Blumen gießen,' 'Es gießt' (It is pouring rain).

A: Can you water the flowers?
B: Sure, they look dry.
A: Water them every evening in summer.
B: Otherwise they will die.
To pour or to water (plants). 'Blumen gießen' (to water flowers). Strong verb: goss, gegossen. 'Es gießt' means 'It is pouring rain.'
The last concert was a single, specific moment in the past.
The verb 'sprechen' pairs with which preposition to mean 'talk about'?
Mia says: Lukas does not talk about his band.
'Denken AN' requires da + an. What is da + an?
First blank: having the band was a specific period in the past. Second blank: playing alone is a repeated present situation.
When Lukas had his band, they played every Friday. When he plays alone now, it sounds different.
The verb is 'denken AN.' The wo-compound combines wo + an.
The word for a meadow where flowers and grass grow.
The flowers on the meadow bloom in summer.
German is not one language but many regional flavors. Learn farming vocabulary, then discover what Austrians and Swiss call their food.
Learn words for farming, residents, and pets
Discover Austrian and Swiss German food vocabulary
Understand regional identity through food words
You know, when I was a child in Salzburg, we never said Kartoffel. Always Erdapfel. And tomatoes? Paradeiser! Berlin trained it out of me, but sometimes it slips out.
Wait. You are from Salzburg? I always thought you were a real Berliner. Hildi, you are also someone who moved here!
the farmer
From Old High German 'giburo' (neighbor). Also the pawn in chess.

A: The farmer sells fresh asparagus.
B: Straight from the field!
A: Farmers' markets are popular in Berlin.
B: Every Saturday at Maybachufer.
From Old High German 'giburo' (neighbor, fellow dweller). Also the pawn in chess. 'Bauernregel' (farmer's rule) = weather proverbs based on centuries of agricultural observation.
the farm
'Bauer' + 'Hof' (yard/farm). Masculine because Hof is masculine.

A: Have you ever been to a farm?
B: Yes, as a child. We vacationed there.
A: Farm vacations are great for children.
B: And for adults!
'Bauer' + 'Hof' (yard/farm). 'Urlaub auf dem Bauernhof' (vacation on a farm) is a popular German family holiday.
the resident, the inhabitant
From 'bewohnen' (to inhabit). The Be- prefix + wohnen = who lives somewhere.

A: How many residents does the building have?
B: Twelve parties.
A: Does everyone know each other?
B: Most of them, yes. We barbecue together.
From 'bewohnen' (to inhabit). 'Die Bewohner des Hauses' (the residents of the building). The Be- prefix + wohnen makes it about who lives somewhere.
the resident (female)
Bewohner + -in. On official forms: 'Bewohner/Bewohnerin.'

A: Are you a resident of this building?
B: Yes, for five years.
A: Then you surely know Mrs. Schmidt.
B: Of course, she lives above me.
Female resident. On official forms: 'Bewohner/Bewohnerin' or the modern 'Bewohnende' (gender-neutral).
the tenant
From 'mieten' (to rent). Over 50% of Germans rent rather than own.

A: The tenant on the third floor is moving out.
B: Too bad, he was nice.
A: Who will be the new tenant?
B: No idea, but demand is high.
From 'mieten' (to rent). Germany is a nation of renters: over 50% of Germans rent rather than own their home. Der Mieter has strong legal protections.
the tenant (female)
Mieter + -in. German rental law is among the most tenant-friendly in the world.

A: The tenant complained.
B: About what?
A: About the noise from the beer garden.
B: Understandable, but it is summer!
Female tenant. German rental law (Mietrecht) is among the most tenant-friendly in the world. Evictions are difficult. Rent caps exist.
the pet
'Haus' (house) + 'Tier' (animal). Neuter because Tier is neuter.

A: Do you have a pet?
B: Yes, a cat.
A: Pets are usually allowed in beer gardens.
B: My cat prefers to stay home.
'Haus' (house) + 'Tier' (animal). Germans love their Haustiere: 34 million pets, more than in most European countries. Dogs can ride public transit.
Austrian for 'Aprikose.' From Italian 'amarella' (little bitter one).

A: In Vienna they say Marille.
B: And in Berlin Aprikose.
A: Apricot dumplings are Austrian.
B: And incredibly delicious!
Austrian for 'Aprikose' (apricot). From Italian 'amarella' (little bitter one). 'Marillenknödel' (apricot dumplings) are Austria's most famous dessert.
the tomato (Austrian)
Austrian for 'Tomate.' From 'Paradiesapfel' (paradise apple).

A: Please pass me a Paradeiser.
B: You mean a tomato?
A: In Austria we say Paradeiser.
B: Paradise apple, how poetic!
Austrian for 'Tomate.' From Italian 'pomodoro' (golden apple) via 'Paradiesapfel' (paradise apple). Austrians still call the tomato a 'paradise fruit.'
the potato (Austrian/Swiss)
'Erd' (earth) + 'Apfel' (apple). French says the same: 'pomme de terre.'

A: Potato salad with the schnitzel?
B: Absolutely! In Austria they belong together.
A: Kartoffelsalat in Germany.
B: Erdapfelsalat in Vienna. Same dish.
Austrian/Swiss for 'Kartoffel' (potato). 'Erd' (earth) + 'Apfel' (apple). An earth apple. French says the same: 'pomme de terre.' Three names for one vegetable.
the chicken (Austrian)
Austrian diminutive. The -erl suffix is the Austrian form of -chen.

A: Half a chicken, please!
B: With potato salad?
A: Of course!
B: Coming right up.
Austrian dialect for a small chicken. The -erl suffix is the Austrian diminutive (like -chen in standard German). 'Ein halbes Henderl' is a classic Austrian Gasthaus order.
the chicken (Bavarian)
Bavarian dialect. Even shorter than Henderl. Neuter because of the diminutive.

A: At Oktoberfest there is chicken.
B: Chicken with potato salad!
A: The classic Oktoberfest meal.
B: With a liter of beer.
Bavarian dialect for chicken. Even shorter than Henderl. 'Hendl mit Kartoffelsalat' is the quintessential Oktoberfest meal.
the cream (Austrian)
Austrian for 'Sahne.' From 'das Obere' (the top part) because cream rises to the top.

A: Coffee with cream, please.
B: In Germany they say Sahne.
A: But Obers sounds nicer.
B: I think so too!
Austrian for 'Sahne' (cream). From 'das Obere' (the top part) because cream rises to the top of milk. 'Kaffee mit Obers' is how you order coffee with cream in Vienna.
the mushroom (Bavarian/Austrian)
The -erl diminutive. 'Schwammerlsuppe' (mushroom soup) is Bavarian comfort food.

A: Let us go mushroom hunting!
B: In the forest? Is that not dangerous?
A: Not if you know what you are doing.
B: Mushroom soup would be great.
Bavarian/Austrian for 'Pilz' (mushroom). 'Schwammerlsuppe' (mushroom soup) is comfort food in Bavaria and Austria. Standard German 'Pilz' sounds clinical by comparison.
the butcher (Austrian)
'Fleisch' (meat) + 'Hauer' (one who chops). The word paints a picture.

A: Where is the nearest butcher?
B: In Austria they say Fleischhauer.
A: In Germany Metzger or Fleischer.
B: Three words, one trade.
Austrian for 'Metzger' (butcher). 'Fleisch' (meat) + 'Hauer' (one who chops). Standard German has Metzger (south) and Fleischer (north). Three words, one trade.
the oven (Austrian)
'Back' (bake) + 'Rohr' (pipe/tube). The oven as a baking tube.

A: Put the cake in the oven!
B: At what temperature?
A: 180 degrees, as always.
B: In Germany they say Backofen.
Austrian for 'Backofen' (oven). 'Back' (bake) + 'Rohr' (pipe/tube). The oven as a baking tube. Same appliance, Austrian name.
the bread roll (Swiss)
Swiss diminutive. The -li suffix is the Swiss form of -chen.

A: A bread roll with butter, please.
B: In Zurich they say Brötli.
A: In Berlin Brötchen, in Munich Semmel.
B: Every region has its own word!
Swiss German for 'Brötchen' (bread roll). The -li suffix is the Swiss diminutive. Every German-speaking country has its own word for a bread roll.
the ice cream (Swiss)
Swiss German for 'Eis.' From French 'glace' (ice). Shows French influence.

A: Would you like a scoop of ice cream?
B: In Switzerland they say it that way.
A: A scoop of Glace in Bern, a scoop of Eis in Berlin.
B: The main thing is it tastes good!
Swiss German for 'Eis' (ice cream). From French 'glace' (ice). Switzerland's French influence shows in its food vocabulary.

German is spoken in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland (plus Liechtenstein, Luxembourg, and parts of Belgium and Italy). Each country has unique food vocabulary.

Standard German is understood everywhere, but regional terms carry identity:

Tomate (DE) = Paradeiser (AT)
Kartoffel (DE) = Erdapfel (AT/CH)
Sahne (DE) = Obers (AT)
Brötchen (DE) = Semmel (BY/AT) = Brötli (CH)
Eis (DE) = Glace (CH)
Pilz (DE) = Schwammerl (BY/AT)

The Biergarten is Bavarian. The Beisl is Austrian. The Beiz is Swiss. Same concept: a place to eat, drink, and belong.

Knowing regional variants shows respect and cultural awareness. Austrian German (Österreichisches Deutsch) and Swiss Standard German (Schweizer Hochdeutsch) are officially recognized standard varieties, not dialects. They have their own dictionaries and official vocabulary lists.

Three countries, three languages, one asparagus. Verumius secretly writes a list on a napkin: Paradeiser, Erdapfel, Obers, Schwammerl. The German on the menu is not enough. You need at least three menus.
This word comes from 'das Obere' because cream rises to the top of the milk.
The Austrian word for cream, from 'the top part' of milk.
In Vienna you order 'coffee with Obers' instead of 'coffee with Sahne.'
Warten always pairs with this preposition. You wait ON something.
Being in Vienna was a specific trip in the past. One time.
Time to prove your skills. Review all Unit 18 grammar, vocabulary, and cultural knowledge in a comprehensive assessment.
Test als vs wenn in varied contexts
Prove mastery of verbs with fixed prepositions
Use da-/wo- compounds fluently
The evening draws on. Fairy lights glow brighter as the sky grows darker. Hildi raises her beer stein. The others follow. Six glasses clink at once. Eye contact, as is proper.
the abbot
From Latin 'abbas.' Many German breweries were founded by monks.

A: The abbot led the monastery.
B: And the brewery!
A: Monastery beer has a long tradition.
B: Some monasteries still brew today.
From Latin 'abbas.' Many German breweries were founded by monks, and the Abt supervised beer production. 'Klosterbier' (monastery beer) has a centuries-old tradition.
the caretaker (Swiss)
Swiss German for 'Hausmeister.' 'Ab' (away) + 'Wart' (one who watches).

A: The caretaker takes care of the building.
B: In Germany they say Hausmeister.
A: In Switzerland it is the Abwart.
B: Another regional word!
Swiss German for 'Hausmeister' (caretaker/janitor). 'Ab' (away) + 'Wart' (one who watches). The person who watches over a building.
the caretaker (female, Swiss)
Abwart + -in. Swiss German follows the same -in pattern for feminine forms.

A: The caretaker cleaned the hallway.
B: She does that every morning.
A: In Switzerland they say Abwartin.
B: And in Germany Hausmeisterin.
Female caretaker. Swiss German follows the same -in pattern for feminine forms as standard German.
the boy (Bavarian/Austrian/Swiss)
Southern German for 'Junge.' Used across all southern German-speaking regions.

A: The boy is playing in the garden.
B: In Bavaria they say Bub.
A: In northern Germany they say Junge.
B: North-south difference, as always.
Bavarian/Austrian/Swiss for 'Junge' (boy). 'Ein lieber Bub.' In northern Germany, nobody says Bub. Another north-south divide.
February (Austrian)
Austrian for 'Februar.' Shortened, localized. Instantly marks someone as Austrian.

A: In Feber it is cold in Vienna.
B: Feber? You mean February?
A: In Austria we say Feber.
B: Learned something new!
Austrian for 'Februar.' One of those calendar words that instantly tells you someone is Austrian.
the chicken (Swiss)
From French 'poulet.' Switzerland's French influence again.

A: Chicken with fries, please.
B: In Zurich they say Poulet.
A: In Berlin Hähnchen, in Munich Hendl.
B: Three countries, three chickens!
Swiss German for 'Hähnchen' (chicken). From French 'poulet.' 'Poulet mit Pommes' in Zurich = 'Hähnchen mit Pommes' in Berlin = 'Hendl mit Pommes' in Munich.
a few, some
Written lowercase. 'Ein Paar' (capitalized) = a pair/couple. Case matters!

A: I need a few minutes.
B: No problem, take your time.
A: A few friends are coming later.
B: The more, the merrier!
Written lowercase (unlike 'ein Paar' = a pair/couple). 'Ein paar Freunde' = a few friends. 'Ein Paar Schuhe' = a pair of shoes. The capital P changes the meaning entirely.
just, simply (particle)
'Das ist halt so.' Extremely common in spoken German, especially southern.

A: Why does it rain so much?
B: That is just how summer is in Berlin.
A: I wanted to sit in the beer garden.
B: Then you just have to wait.
A particle meaning 'just, simply.' 'Das ist halt so' (That is just how it is). Extremely common in spoken German, especially southern. Signals acceptance or resignation.
extra, on purpose, especially
Same as English. Also means 'on purpose': 'Das hast du extra gemacht!'

A: I bought asparagus especially for you.
B: How nice of you!
A: The last of the season.
B: Especially for me? Thanks!
Same as English 'extra.' Also means 'on purpose': 'Das hast du extra gemacht!' (You did that on purpose!).
free (of charge)
From Latin 'gratis.' Also 'kostenlos' or 'umsonst.'

A: Is admission free?
B: Yes, the beer garden is freely accessible.
A: And the water?
B: Also free, with the beer.
From Latin 'gratis' (for free). 'Der Eintritt ist gratis.' Germans also say 'kostenlos' (costless) or 'umsonst' (for nothing).
every time
'Jedes' (every) + 'Mal' (time). Perfect with 'wenn' for repeated actions.

A: Every time I am here, I order asparagus.
B: You are like Heinrich!
A: Every time it tastes different.
B: That makes it exciting.
'Jedes' (every) + 'Mal' (time/instance). 'Jedes Mal, wenn ich im Biergarten bin, bestelle ich Spargel.' Perfect with 'wenn' for repeated actions.
on the side, incidentally, by the way
'Neben' (beside) + 'bei' (by). A useful conversational filler.

A: By the way, the asparagus was fantastic.
B: I think so too!
A: And incidentally: Lukas played great.
B: Yes, really impressive.
'Neben' (beside) + 'bei' (by). 'Nebenbei bemerkt...' (By the way...). Also 'nebenbei arbeiten' (to work on the side). A useful conversational filler.
ouch, ow
An exclamation of pain or surprise. Short for 'Aua!' Also a place name element.

A: Ouch! That was hot!
B: Careful with the grill!
A: Ow, my finger!
B: Let me see, is it bad?
An exclamation of pain or surprise. 'Au! Das war heiß!' Short for 'Aua!' Also a place name element: 'Au' means a water meadow.
the lightning, the flash
Also 'Blitzkrieg' (lightning war), 'Blitzeis' (flash ice). Fast and sudden.

A: Did you see the lightning?
B: Yes! And then the thunder!
A: A thunderstorm in the beer garden.
B: Quick, under the roof!
Same word as English 'blitz.' 'Blitzschnell' (lightning fast), 'Blitzeis' (flash ice on roads). Also the camera flash. Germans say 'Blitz' when a summer storm interrupts a Biergarten evening.

KD puts down his glass and says quietly: 'Every time we sit here, you order asparagus. Every time you argue about dialects. And every time someone says Prost as if it were a discovery.' Mia laughs. 'That is called tradition, KD.'
'Jedes Mal' signals repetition. Which 'when' word is for repeated events?
Which verb means 'to insist' and pairs with 'auf'?
The verb is 'sich interessieren FÜR.' Replace 'für Bio-Essen' with da + für.
Mia is interested in organic food. She is interested in it.
The wo-compound wo + über asks about the topic of a feeling. Which preposition does 'sich freuen' pair with for current feelings?
First: one-time past event. Second: happy ABOUT it (past, da + über). Third + fourth: every time, looking forward to it (future, da + auf).
When I ate asparagus for the first time, I was happy about it. Now I look forward to it every time.
This word comes from French and refers to poultry.
A word from Latin meaning 'for free.'
Admission to the beer garden is free. You do not have to pay anything.
'Warten AUF' is the fixed combo. Question = wo + auf. Answer = da + auf.
First blank: one specific past moment (als vs. wenn). Second blank: bestehen takes a fixed preposition. Replace 'auf etwas' with the da-compound.
When we were sitting in the beer garden, Heinrich called. He insisted that we order asparagus.
Berlin summer night. The group walks home together, splitting off one by one. Final vocabulary, final grammar assessment, and a moment of belonging.
Learn remaining vocabulary for food, farming, and daily life
Master all three grammar constructs in combination
Reflect on friendship and belonging
Berlin summer night. The group spills out of the beer garden. Streetlights glow through linden trees. The air is warm. Mia checks her phone for the last U-Bahn.
That was a lovely evening. We should do this more often. Every time we meet, it feels like home.
When my husband was still alive, we went to the beer garden every summer. I had looked forward to being there again today.
Thanks for today. I have not played in a long time. I am happy that you listened.
Verumius walks the last meters alone. The building entrance is ahead. Hildi's light is already on upstairs. He thinks: 'When I came to Berlin, I knew no one. Now someone is waiting for me.'
the butcher
Standard southern German. Northern: 'Fleischer.' Austrian: 'Fleischhauer.' Three words, one trade.

A: I am going to the butcher.
B: Please bring bratwurst!
A: From the butcher or the supermarket?
B: From the butcher, it tastes better.
Standard southern German. Northern Germans say 'Fleischer,' Austrians say 'Fleischhauer.' From Latin 'macellarius' (slaughterer). Every region defends its word.
to nourish, to feed
Reflexive: 'sich ernähren' (to eat, to sustain oneself). From 'Nahrung.'

A: How do you eat?
B: I eat mostly vegetarian.
A: That is healthy.
B: And good for the environment.
Reflexive: 'sich ernähren' (to eat, to sustain oneself). 'Sich gesund ernähren' (to eat healthily). The er- prefix makes it about achieving nourishment.
to eat (for animals)
Using 'fressen' for a person is rude. Humans 'essen,' animals 'fressen.'

A: The cat eats its food.
B: She always eats so fast.
A: Never say 'fressen' about a person!
B: Why not?
A: That is very rude.
To eat (for animals). 'Der Hund frisst.' Using 'fressen' for a person is rude: 'Er frisst wie ein Schwein' (He eats like a pig). The distinction matters.
to feed (an animal or baby)
From 'Futter' (animal food/feed). 'Die Enten füttern' (to feed the ducks).

A: Can we feed the ducks?
B: Yes, but only with oat flakes.
A: No bread?
B: No, bread is bad for ducks.
From 'Futter' (animal food/feed). Also slang: 'Ich muss meinen Computer füttern' (I need to feed data into my computer).
to barbecue (Swiss)
Swiss German for 'grillen.' The -ieren suffix shows French influence.

A: Shall we barbecue this weekend?
B: In Switzerland they say grillieren.
A: In Germany grillen.
B: Same smoke, different verb!
Swiss German for 'grillen.' The -ieren suffix shows French influence. In Switzerland: 'Wir grillieren am See.' In Germany: 'Wir grillen im Park.'
to sew
From Old High German 'najen.' Related to English 'needle.'

A: Can you sew?
B: Yes, I sew my own buttons on.
A: I need a sewing machine.
B: For big projects it is worth it.
To sew. From Old High German 'najen.' Related to English 'needle' through the same root. 'Mit heißer Nadel genäht' (sewn with a hot needle) means done hastily.
to be useful, to use
Southern/Austrian variant of 'nutzen.' Both are correct.

A: That is no use.
B: Yes it is, it helps more than you think.
A: In Austria they say nützen.
B: In Germany also nutzen.
Southern German and Austrian variant of 'nutzen.' Both are correct. The ü vs u distinction is regional, not grammatical.
to use, to make use of
Standard German variant. 'Die Gelegenheit nutzen' (to seize the opportunity).

A: Seize the opportunity!
B: Which opportunity?
A: The last asparagus of the season!
B: You are right, I will seize it.
'Die Gelegenheit nutzen' (to seize the opportunity). 'Nutzen' as a noun (der Nutzen) means benefit. Verb and noun share the same form.
to throw down
'Runter' (down) + 'werfen' (to throw). Separable. Colloquial contraction of 'herunterwerfen.'

A: Do not throw that down!
B: Sorry, it slipped out of my hand.
A: The beer stein almost fell down.
B: Luckily not!
'Runter' (down) + 'werfen' (to throw). Separable. Colloquial contraction of 'herunterwerfen.' Spoken German loves shortening.
to cater, to provide food and care
From 'Pflege' (care) + ver- prefix. 'Die Gäste verpflegen.'

A: Who caters for the guests today?
B: The chef and his cook.
A: The catering was excellent.
B: Yes, everything fresh and delicious.
From 'Pflege' (care) + ver- prefix. 'Verpflegung' (catering/provisions) appears on conference schedules. The word covers providing complete food and care.
organic, biological (prefix)
Prefix meaning organic. 'Bio-Markt,' 'Bio-Bauer,' 'Bio-Milch.' Legally protected in the EU.

A: Do you shop at the organic market?
B: Yes, mostly organic vegetables.
A: Organic products are more expensive.
B: But the quality is worth it.
Prefix meaning organic or biological. In German, bio- attaches to almost any food noun. The prefix alone on a label means certified organic under EU regulation.
single, individual (prefix)
'Einzelzimmer' (single room), 'Einzelkind' (only child), 'Einzelhandel' (retail).

A: I need a single room.
B: For how many nights?
A: Three. And please no single supplement.
B: No problem!
Prefix meaning single or individual. 'Einzelzimmer' (single room), 'Einzelkind' (only child), 'Einzelhandel' (retail). One of German's most productive compound prefixes.
Arriving in Berlin was a single event in the past.
Darüber expands to da + a preposition. Which preposition does 'sich freuen' pair with for current emotions?
Bestehen AUF. The question form uses wo + auf.
What does Heinrich insist on? -- That we order asparagus.
First: specific past evening (als). Second: happy ABOUT the evening (da + über). Third: had looked FORWARD to it (da + auf).
When we were in the beer garden, Hildi was happy about it. She had looked forward to it for a long time.
This reflexive verb is about sustained, healthy eating. Not for animals.
Every year = repeated (wenn). Thinking OF something = denken AN.
Warten AUF. Then replace 'auf den Frühling' with the da-compound.
Verumius waits for spring. He looks forward to it.
